It's not that they don't care at all.

It's just not their priority, which they've made very clear. They do respect life itself though, and don't like cruelty.

The Keeper understands Necrovia better and respects the way it works, but its business is its own. Otherwise the rest is only part of the deal with Doresain, for their mutual benefit.

Against, it's not that they don't care, it's just not part of their deal, *especially* when their life is constantly under threat and, like has been said above, right now what they wanna do is find William and go home.
The Keeper is an empathetic and kind person at heart, but they're not what people would call an "All loving Hero", especially in such a dangerous world.
Which is not inconsistent or mutually exclusive with the fact that they *do* care.
I think most people would be like that. It’s too hard to care for an entire world easier to care for select groups/person

I do find it to be inconsistent, truthfully. Especially combined with their visceral seeking of vengeance for William (when they thought he had been murdered) - it's not a character I dislike per se, but it's a very different character attribute. Firstly, if they wished to avoid threats to their life when possible, starting a fight to the death over a corpse is no good way to pursue that.

Secondly and more importantly, throughout Arc 1 especially; even in cases of beings who've succeeded in killing many humans during the course of attempting to succeed in their plans, the Keeper has never tried to punish them after they've been stopped. 

Much less pursue righteous vengeance against them, I would describe their past attitude as "What's done is done and a corpse is a corpse, although we should try our hardest to retrieve their Silver". If they'd go after Doresain even if he had done what they interpreted them to, then they absolutely should've been happy for Juliette to die for their actions, which they weren't. Or tried to have Miryam destroyed (again). 

On a similar basis, I find it bizarre that they'd be so ambivalent about Necrovia's fate when the obvious consequence of the Seafarers and Lantern Church (in this dimension) obtaining many thousands of silver cores will be the later death of many humans (even if all of the Ghouls' deaths before then are considered to be "worthless"), or possibly even dissolution that goes beyond the boundaries of the dimension they are in. It is certainly something that doesn't make much sense to shrug off in its scale compared to Salvador's church or Clementine's asylum, both cases where they voluntarily put themselves in a lot of danger mostly to save "just" a few dozen humans.
Oh true that

That 

The story has been very clear that William's murder had a terrible impact on the Keeper and their psyche
also Keeper doesn’t have that 1-2 years to appreciate the world like in arc 1
They way they react in Arc II is very human, including being more "unpredictable"

That's part of human nature under emotions
so ofc Keeper is different compared to arc 1
the environment is simply different
But the Keeper is not fully a human, and even many humans would be capable of appraising the consequences in this situation.

even if we have downtime in arc 2 there is no bakery that actually still exist
there is no lemon cupcake 
there is no Ogier shouting in the training yard
GDoll will never be Doll
For all we know, while they're not fully human at the base, their consciousness has very much developed as a human
but in Keeper mind they’re a human being
even after accepting Tohu truth they still consider themselves as human

In Arc 1 they often demonstrated not really having a "general" human nature, mostly in how they respond to others both in their care and their being deadened. I do agree that Arc 2 is increasingly stressful for them and that can make them act more irrationally, but it still just feels odd given the battles they fought voluntarily in the first two chapters.
It could be theorized that Tohu had the subconscious want to experience "humanity" for themselves
Also, this might've been influenced by the current GPT translation 💀 when I read Chapter 7, but William's murder wasn't really shown to have that gross of an effect upon the Keeper at that time.
Though I get that they kind of had no choice but to keep going on given what was about to happen, so it's hard to say how deeply that knife dug into them.

Honestly I never got that vibe from Arc I

Many of the Keeper's reactions felt right in the range of what someone still developing as a person in an unfamiliar land would do or feel, and didn't strike me as odd
And well, humans can be weird

Gpt might be partly to blame 

But even at the same it was pretty evident the Keeper was seething with rage and grief at what had happened 

But there was no time to linger on it longer
There's also the text message with Tawil where they discuss emotions, and the Keeper intentionally discusses it as a human, as the question is about human emotions and the way human behaves
So they already fully admnit having at least "become" entirely "human" at that point

In Arc 1, to me, the things the Keeper cared deeply about and the things the Keeper hated felt very arbitrary. Heck, with Thais for example they wouldn't even have the same feelings expressed towards her from one conversation to the next (though I admit their past-incarnation bonding and her... proclivities are traumatic, of course). 

They forgave Miryam (enough) but scorned Faros. They didn't bother actively going after the Sculptor's Guild. There's a lot of examples. Overall, I saw the Keeper as being a very passive being, so passive in fact that they'd even absorb the emotions of those addressing them at the time in a lot of cases.

Though I certainly would agree that they've always wanted to be human, I just see the "wanting to be" and being human to be two different things. But that's perspective, in a way. In any case, I'd be perfectly fine with the Keeper having a more active personality and active decision-making from now on, as long as it stays relastively consistent; in almost all of the past chapters (Arc 1 and 2) they've been very reactive only.
Anyway, the biggest culprit behind why the Keeper behaves the way they do in Arc II is the entire situation and environment
also Necrovia and Doresain are entirely alien to Keeper prior his decision to avenge for William
new people, an entirely prejudiced race, and they (suspectedly) murdered William
Keeper also displayed anguish over the thoughts that they couldn't save Sarah in arc 2, something they had failed in arc 1
and she's mostly stranger
William have deeper bond with Keeper, so it still make sense that they just snap at Doresain for (suspectedly) killed William and then feast on his corpse as if its a world-class delicacy

I mean, Thais is...confusing and alien. She's so much like this that the Keeper doesn't really know how to feel besides being confused, which I totally get 😅

For Myriam idk how much they've truly "forgiven", they're pretty insisting on asking Myriam if she regrets, which I imagine is frustrating when they hear her response, as "understandable" as it may be.

I think the Keeper's speech in the epilogue, and their conversation with Ramona expresses it the best: what they want at core is very much a "passive" and tranquil life. Wasting away with the people they love, doing the "meaningless" things that give life meaning. 

But they don't have that luxury, and are thrown in such a violent world after one year of existence that it's hard for them to fully grasp everything.

So yes, the way they react is very human, in their own Keeper way.

All of this.

The Keeper has always cared, but not only do they care more for people that are closer to them (which isn't surprising), they don't always have the *time* to show they care for everyone they do for in the first place, like for Sarah who they actually truly wanted to save, and feel guilt and grief for not being able to
The situation/environment is just very awful, constant pressure and danger pretty much everywhere all the time.
